wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The names match exactly. The Wikidata entity is a known artist born in 1941, which means she would have been 28 years old at the time of the 1969 card entry, fitting the profile of an active artist during that period.

results.possibleIdentity: Adrianne Wortzel (born October 7, 1941, in Brooklyn, NY) is a pioneering American media artist, roboticist, and educator who has been active in the art world for over six decades. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, Wortzel's early career focused on abstract painting and sculpture. She studied at the Brooklyn Museum Art School and later at Brooklyn College (B.A. 1963) under influential figures such as Ad Reinhardt, Jimmy Ernst, and Louise Bourgeois. In the mid-1960s, she began experimenting with technology-infused art, creating geometric 3D sculptures (precursors to her later time-based work) that were motorized to appear as if they were "breathing." This experimental approach aligned her with the mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; historical documents and scholarly texts on the history of electronic art frequently associate her name with the E.A.T. community and the development of art as an experimental research practice involving scientists and engineers. In the 1990s, she transitioned significantly into new media, earning an MFA in Computer Arts from the School of Visual Arts (1996) and becoming a pioneer in telerobotics and robotic theater. Her notable works include 'Camouflage Town' (2001), a telerobotic installation at the Whitney Museum of American Art, and 'The Electronic Chronicles' (1995), one of the earliest fictive works on the web. Wortzel is a Professor Emerita in the Departments of Emerging Media Technologies and Entertainment Technologies at the New York City College of Technology (CUNY).

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Fiala was an artist, choreographer, and director active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, primarily known for his leadership in the Multigravitational Experimental Group (also known as the Multigravitational Group or MGG). Founded in 1971 alongside Stephanie Evanitsky, the group was a New York-based dance and performance collective that specialized in "aerodance" or "air dance." This avant-garde style featured dancers performing on complex scaffolding and suspended apparatuses, effectively bridging the worlds of choreography and engineering. Fiala served as the group's Assistant Artistic Director and later as its Artistic Director. The group's work was highly technical and experimental, often involving collaborations with engineers for lighting and sound—notably composers from Bell Laboratories—which aligns with the mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Significant performances by the group under his direction included appearances at the Grand Palais in Paris in 1972 and at various venues in New York City.

results.possibleIdentity: Phyllis Gershuny (now known as Phyllis Segura) is an artist and editor who was a central figure in the early video art movement of the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is best known as a co-founder and the first editor of 'Radical Software', the first periodical dedicated to independent video, which she launched in 1970 alongside Beryl Korot and Ira Schneider. Gershuny conceptualized the magazine as an 'evolving handbook of technology' and a tool for social change, advocating for decentralized access to information. Before founding the magazine, she lived in Europe where she worked with the Living Theater and was introduced to video technology by John Hopkins. Although she did not produce her own videotapes, she was instrumental in the design and editorial direction of the first three issues of 'Radical Software' before leaving the publication in 1971. In her later career, she returned to painting and relocated to the Hudson River area. She is also recognized as a personal chef and the founder of the poetry magazine 'Broadshirt'. Her work and influence are often cited in the context of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and other organizations that merged art with scientific innovation during that era.

results.possibleIdentity: Davidson Gigliotti (born 1939) is a pioneering American video artist and art historian who was a central figure in the early video art movement of the late 1960s and 1970s. He was an original member of the influential video collective Videofreex, founded in 1969, and its later iteration, Media Bus. Gigliotti's work often focused on multi-channel video installations that explored natural and urban environments, with notable pieces including 'Quaking Aspens' (1972) and 'Hunter Mountain' (1973). He was also a significant figure in the SoHo art community, collaborating with artists like Jean Dupuy and Nam June Paik. His association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is documented by his own account; he sought technical assistance from the organization, which led to a pivotal professional referral to CBS and subsequently to the formation of the Videofreex. Gigliotti served as the Director of Video at the Experimental Intermedia Foundation in New York from 1975 to 1987 and is widely recognized for his work as a historian of early video art, maintaining extensive archives known as 'Davidson's Files.'

results.possibleIdentity: Joel Heathcote (often spelled Heathcote, but referred to in some contexts with variations) is a lighting artist, designer, and technical collaborator who has been active since the 1960s. He graduated from Dartmouth College in 1961 and became a prominent figure in the intersection of art and technology, specializing in "lighting effects," lasers, fiber optics, and kinetic colors. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was involved in experimental multi-media projects and documentation; for instance, in 1974, he was noted for donating services to the Philosophical Research Society by creating 35mm slides of their rare art and books. Heathcote is perhaps best known for his long-term involvement with Buckminster Fuller's "The World Game" project, where he served as a lighting artist and designer for multi-media events, including major presentations at UCSD and SDSU. His work often involved the use of advanced technology in architectural and performative contexts, such as using "fiber optics, lasers, and kinetic colors in architecture." He is also a long-standing member of the Casque and Gauntlet society at Dartmouth, where he was dubbed "Merlin of Merlins" for his decades of designing "ritual light magic" and installations. His interdisciplinary career as a lighting designer and technical artist aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization active during his most productive years that facilitated collaborations between artists and engineers.

While Weiss's specific artistic collaborations are not documented in the provided snippets, his role as a research engineer at the specific institution that birthed and supported E.A.T. makes him a likely candidate for the technical 'artist-engineer' identity sought.

results.possibleIdentity: Susan Alexander (also known as Susan Alexjander) is a composer, sound designer, and biomusician who is recognized for her pioneering collaborations with scientists and engineers to translate scientific data into sound. Her work, which she describes as "molecular music," involves mapping the frequencies of natural phenomena—such as DNA sequences, atomic elements (Larmor frequencies), pulsars, and black holes—into audible frequencies played through synthesizers. She is noted for her 1988 project "Sequencia," a collaboration with cell biologist Dr. David Deamer that used DNA base pairs to create music. Her work is frequently cited in the context of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and other art-science labs (such as the Center for Advanced Visual Studies at MIT) because of her focus on interdisciplinary synthesis. She has been active since at least the late 1960s or early 1970s; records indicate a Susan Alexander was active as a performer and composer in musical productions as early as 1968. She has collaborated with various visual artists, including an installation with Rebecca Kamen titled "Divining Nature: An Expanded Periodic Table," which featured her science-based soundscapes.

Susan Alexander Speeth was a microbiologist and community activist in Philadelphia during the early 1970s who was connected to the experimental and avant-garde art scene of that era. She is historically significant for making a large donation to support the production of "Escalator Over the Hill," an avant-garde jazz opera by Carla Bley and Paul Haines recorded between 1968 and 1971, which involved numerous experimental artists and collaborators. Speeth was tragically murdered in October 1975, an event that became a primary impetus for the international "Take Back the Night" movement against sexual and gendered violence. While her primary profession was in the sciences (microbiology), her financial and social support for radical artistic projects in the late 1960s and 1970s places her within the circle of art-technology-science collaborations characteristic of the period.

results.possibleIdentity: Daina Krumins (born 1947) is an American experimental filmmaker, photographer, and surrealist artist who has been active since the late 1960s. Born in a Munich refugee camp, she immigrated to the United States as a child and later earned a BFA from the NYU Film School followed by an MFA from the California Institute of the Arts (CalArts). She is best known for her painstakingly produced avant-garde films, including the award-winning 'The Divine Miracle' (1973), 'Babobilicons' (1982), and 'Summer Light' (2001). Her work often focuses on intricate textures and biological phenomena, such as slime molds and mushrooms, and she identifies as a neurodivergent artist with Asperger's syndrome. Professionally, she was a collaborator of filmmaker Pat O'Neill at Lookout Mountain Films for five years, where she worked as a rotoscoper and optical printer operator. Her technical skills led to work on major Hollywood productions, such as painting auras for the film 'The Empire Strikes Back'. Krumins was also associated with the early New York experimental scene at The Kitchen in the early 1970s, an environment deeply connected to the 'Experiments in Art and Technology' (E.A.T.) movement and emergent electronic arts.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael William "Mike" Hart (1943–2016) was an English singer-songwriter and poet who was a central figure in the Liverpool underground arts scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known as a member of The Liverpool Scene, a multidisciplinary poetry and music collective formed in 1967 that included Adrian Henri, Roger McGough, and Andy Roberts. The group was noted for its "skilful fusion" of pop, folk, jazz, and blues with avant-garde performance art. After leaving the group in 1968, Hart released two solo albums, "Mike Hart Bleeds" (1969) and "Basher, Chalky, Pongo and Me" (1972), both of which were produced by John Peel on his Dandelion Records label. His work, such as the song "Almost Liverpool 8," was highly regarded by Peel and remains a notable example of the era's experimental counter-culture art.

Michael Stern Hart (1947–2011) was a technologist and visionary who founded Project Gutenberg in 1971 while at the University of Illinois. Although primarily known as the inventor of the electronic book (e-book), Hart is frequently cited in the history of "Art and Technology" for his concept of "Replicator Technology," which posits that any item capable of being digitized can be reproduced indefinitely. His pioneering work in the early 1970s is often analyzed alongside the activities of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in scholarly discussions regarding the fusion of artistic collaboration and automated digital labor during the late 20th century.

Professor Michael Hart is a distinguished physicist active since the 1960s who may be the scientist identity associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1965, along with Ulrich Bonse, he developed the Bonse-Hart X-ray interferometer, a tool that revolutionized X-ray phase-contrast imaging. E.A.T. was a non-profit organization that specifically paired artists with engineers and scientists to promote collaboration, and technologies like interferometry and holography were major areas of focus for E.A.T. projects (such as those at the Osaka Pepsi Pavilion). Hart held senior positions at Bristol University, Daresbury Laboratory, and the National Synchrotron Light Source (NSLS).

results.possibleIdentity: Richard Lowenberg (born 1946, Haifa) is an American media artist, designer, and eco-cultural activist whose career has been defined by the creative integration of art, science, and technology. Active since the mid-1960s, he studied architecture and design at the Pratt Institute in New York before moving to the San Francisco Bay Area in 1968. Lowenberg is a notable pioneer in bio-sensing art and telecommunication art. He was a founding member of The Kitchen in New York City (1971) and has been associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), having become a member through co-founder Robert Whitman. His work often explores the 'ecology of the information environment.' Major projects include his collaborations with NASA (1974–1984), such as the 'NASA-Arts Project' and 'Gravitational Field Day' (1979), as well as his work on the feature film 'The Secret Life of Plants' (1976–1979), where he used bio-telemetry to translate plant physiology into audio-visual output. He also founded the Bio-Arts Lab (1974–1985) and the Telluride Institute (1984). His multidisciplinary practice encompasses photography, film, video synthesis, performance, and environmental design.
